coredata.dc:title: Benzothiazole derivative-functionalized carbon dots for the detection of berberine hydrochloride
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Fanyong
Sun, Zhonghui
Luo, Shiying
Zhang, Hao
coredata.dc:description: Berberine hydrochloride (BRH) has a wide range of biomedical values, including antioxidant, antibacterial and anti-cancer, so it is very important to establish a rapid and effective method for the determination of BRH. Herein, the fluorescent CDs with rich carboxyl groups on the surface were synthesized by the facile one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and methionine. The surface of CDs was further modified with 2-(4-aminophenyl)benzothiazole (ABT) to obtain CDs-ABT. The emission generated by the surface energy trap becomes more stable after surface modification, so CDs-ABT (31%) has a larger quantum yield than CDs (25%). In addition, BRH can significantly quench the fluorescence of CDs-ABT through inner filter effect (IFE). Experiments were carried out by using central composite design (CCD) and response surface method (RSM) and the best conditions for detecting BRH were obtained. Excellent linear correlation between the BRH concentration in the range of 10–130 μM and the fluorescence quenching of CDs-ABT was observed, and the limit of detection down to 3.5 nM. The assay was successfully applied to the detection of BRH in actual drug samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Functionalized carbon dots
Berberine hydrochloride detection
Inner filter effect
Central composite design
Response surface method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of BRH in PBS buffer solution
Fluorescence detection of CDs-ABT to BRH
Detection mechanism of CDs-ABT to BRH
Detection of BRH in real samples

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51678409 , 51638011 and 51578375 ), Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology ( 19JCYBJC19800 , 18JCYBJC87500 , 15ZCZDSF00880 ), State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes ( Z1-201507 ), and the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin ( TD13-5042 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CDs (A) and CDs-ABT (B), the inset show the HRTEM images. Particle size distribution analysis of CDs (C) and CDs-ABT (D).
(A) FTIR spectra of CDs, ABT and CDs-ABT. (B) XPS spectra of CDs and CDs-ABT. (C) Zata potential of CDs and CDs-ABT.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, ABT and CDs-ABT. (B) Emission spectra of CDs. Inset: photographs of CDs under daylight (left) and an ultraviolet beam of 365 nm (right). (C) Emission spectra of CDs-ABT. Inset: photographs of CDs-ABT under daylight (left) and an ultraviolet beam of 365 nm (right). (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs-ABT (black line), excitation (blue line) and emission spectrum (red line).
(A) Predicted vs. actual values plot. (B) Normal probability plot of residuals. (C) Run number vs. internally studentized residual plot.
Response surfaces plot for CCD: (A) pH- Time (B) Temperature-Time (C) Temperature-pH.

(A) Fluorescence spectrum of CDs-ABT with the addition of various concentrations of BRH. (B) Linear relationship between the fluorescence response (F0–F) and concentration of BRH from 10 to 130 μM. (C) Influences of BRH and other analytes on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-ABT. Concentrations of all analytes were 130 μM.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs-ABT, BRH and CDs-ABT + BRH. (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of BRH and the maximum excitation and emission spectrum of CDs-ABT. (C)The fluorescence lifetime decay curves of CDs-ABT with and without BRH (100 μM).
Schematic illustration of berberine hydrochloride (BRH) detection using functionalized carbon dots of benzothiazole derivatives (CDs-ABT).
Comparison of the reported typical methods for the detection of BRH.
Determination of BRH in the commercial tablets.

row.entry: Fu fang huang lian su tablets

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of BRH in urine samples.

head.ce:title: Benzothiazole derivative-functionalized carbon dots for the detection of berberine hydrochloride

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes/National Center for International Joint Research on Separation Membranes, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Tiangong University, Tianjin, 300387, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes/National Center for International Joint Research on Separation Membranes
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes/National Center for International Joint Research on Separation Membranes, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Tiangong University, Tianjin, 300387, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Berberine hydrochloride (BRH) has a wide range of biomedical values, including antioxidant, antibacterial and anti-cancer, so it is very important to establish a rapid and effective method for the determination of BRH. Herein, the fluorescent CDs with rich carboxyl groups on the surface were synthesized by the facile one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and methionine. The surface of CDs was further modified with 2-(4-aminophenyl)benzothiazole (ABT) to obtain CDs-ABT. The emission generated by the surface energy trap becomes more stable after surface modification, so CDs-ABT (31%) has a larger quantum yield than CDs (25%). In addition, BRH can significantly quench the fluorescence of CDs-ABT through inner filter effect (IFE). Experiments were carried out by using central composite design (CCD) and response surface method (RSM) and the best conditions for detecting BRH were obtained. Excellent linear correlation between the BRH concentration in the range of 10–130 μM and the fluorescence quenching of CDs-ABT was observed, and the limit of detection down to 3.5 nM. The assay was successfully applied to the detection of BRH in actual drug samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Berberine hydrochloride (BRH) is an isoquinoline alkaloid and the main active ingredient in the plant medicine Coptis chinensis [1–3]. It can be antioxidant, antibacterial, anti-tumor and anti-diabetes [4–6]. Due to the abundant sources of medicines, relatively moderate prices, and few adverse reactions, BRH is widely used as a broad-spectrum antibacterial drug in the modern pharmaceutical industry [7,8]. However, long-term excessive exposure to BRH can cause a serious burden on the human body and increase the risk of certain diseases (such as neonatal jaundice) [9,10]. Therefore, the establishment of a fast and simple method to detect BRH is of great significance for the reasonable control of the content of BRH in drugs. Traditional methods for determining BBR include chemiluminescence, electrochemical analysis, capillary electrophoresis, and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [11–15], but these methods have disadvantages such as long detection cycles, expensive equipment, and high professional experience requirements in industrial production [16–19]. Therefore, in order to realize the real-time monitoring of the BRH content in the extraction process of Rhizoma Coptidis, it is also important to establish a fast and effective method to determine the BRH.

Based on the excellent luminescence performance, good biocompatibility and low toxicity of CDs, it has good application potential in the fields of bioimaging, biochemical sensing and environmental detection. It has become a multi-disciplinary field in chemistry, biology, environment, and physics research hotspots [20–23]. Most of the reported CDs are used to detect metal ions, some anions and macromolecules, and their applications in drug detection are still very limited [24–27]. Unfunctionalized CDs composed of carbon and oxygen are usually limited in their application due to the unity of surface functional groups, lack of diverse active sites and low quantum yield [28–30]. Therefore, the functionalization of CDs (including heteroatom doping and surface modification) is a powerful strategy to expand their application range. Zhang et al. [31] used citric acid and 5-amino-1,10-phenanthroline (Aphen) as raw materials to synthesize Aphen functionalized three-color (blue, green and red) fluorescent emission CDs (AC-CDs) by hydrothermal method. High purity white light emission can be obtained by adjusting the excitation and concentration of AC-CDs. AC-CDs can be used to manufacture white light emitting diodes (WLEDs). The surface modification can more effectively regulate the surface state of CDs. Yuan et al. [32] developed a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe based on naphthalimide derivative-functionalized carbon dots (ND-CDs) for monitoring formaldehyde (FA). Due to the large molecular size and basic amine functionalized surface, CDs-ND can specifically target and stain lysosomes, and detect exogenous and endogenous FA in HeLa cells. Yan et al. [33] prepared functionalized carbon dots of thiazole derivatives (CDs-AP) based on amide coupling reaction. The fluorescence of CDs-AP can be quenched by tetracyclines (TCs) based on IFE, so CDs-AP can be used for the determination of tetracycline (TET, an example of TCs) in milk and cell imaging of MDA-MB-231 cells.

In this work, blue emissive CDs were first synthesized with citric acid and methionine. Afterwards, ABT was successfully modified to the surface of CDs by simpler amidation coupling reaction to obtain CDs-ABT, and a method for detecting BRH based on the fluorescence quenching signal of CDs-ABT was established (Scheme 1 ). The fluorescence quenching mechanism of BRH to CDs-ABT was found to be IFE. Taking into account the interaction between different detection conditions, we used CCD and RSM to study pH, incubation time and temperature, and acquired the optimized operating conditions for the detection of BRH by CDs-ABT. In addition, the potential application of the probe in BRH detection was further verified in Fu fang huang lian su tablets and urine samples.

A simple one-pot hydrothermal method was applied to prepare CDs. First, 1.73 g citric acid and 0.45 g methionine were mixed with 30 mL distilled water. Then, pour the above solution into a 50 mL reactor containing a polytetrafluoroethylene liner and heat it at 200 °C for 5 h. After the solution is naturally cooled to room temperature, filter with a 0.45 μm disposable pinhole filter to obtain a yellow solution. After that, dialysis was performed for 24 h through a dialysis membrane bag (MWCO) = 1000. Finally, the CDs aqueous solution in the dialysis bag was collected and rotary evaporated under vacuum to obtain light yellow solid CDs (1.48 g, 67.9%).

ABT has been synthesized by a reported method [34]. Add p-aminobenzoic acid (2.74 g, 20.0 mmol) and 2-aminothiophenol (2.14 mL, 20.0 mmol) to 30 mL of PPA and stir at 170 °C for 4 h (Fig. S1). After being cooled to room temperature, the reaction mixture was poured into cold distilled water, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7.0 with NaHCO3, and extracted with ethyl acetate. The organic extract was rotary evaporated at 60 °C and recrystallized from ethyl acetate to obtain a pure product (3.81 g, 84%). Melting point: 156-157 °C. 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO‑d 6) δ 7.99 (d, J = 8.4 Hz, 1H), 7.91 (d, J = 7.8 Hz, 1H), 7.79 (d, J = 8.6 Hz, 2H), 7.45 (s, 1H), 7.33 (s, 1H), 6.71 (d, J = 8.6 Hz, 2H), 5.93 (s, 2H) (Fig. S2). Anal. Calcd for C13H10N2S: C, 69.04; H, 4.41; N, 12.36; S, 14.19. Found: C, 69.01; H, 4.43; N, 12.40; S, 14.15. UV–Vis spectra: EtOH solution, λ max, 342 nm.

The classic EDC/NHS reaction was used to prepare CDs-ABT. Under stirring, 0.7 g CDs was first added to 30 mL ethanol, and then 0.8 g EDC was added and stirred for 30 min. Then 0.4 g of NHS and 0.1 g of ABT were added to the solution in sequence. Stir at room temperature in the dark for 28 h and monitor the reaction with TCL. After the reaction, the product was separated and purified by silica gel column chromatography (the eluent was CH2Cl2:CH3OH = 20:1), and finally the eluent was removed with a rotary evaporator to obtain light yellow solid CDs-ABT (0.2 g).

Use Design Expert. 8.0.6 software for experimental design, surface response modeling and optimization condition function calculation. Three variables including pH, incubation time and system temperature were evaluated by RSM based on CCD to affect the fluorescence response (F0–F) [35]. The responses obtained from 20 experimental runs are shown in Table S1. Design-Expert software will automatically fit the model, and then perform analysis of variance (ANOVA) based on the contribution of the residuals to various factors to analyze the obtained results. According to the experimental data, a quadratic polynomial model is used to describe the relationship between the fluorescence response (F0–F) and the three independent variables:
(1)
F 0
− F = + 1202.03281 + 23.14908
X 1
+ 10.06939
X 2
− 0.97251
X 3
+ 6.13125
X 1
X 2
− 8.11275
X 1
X 3
− 20.61025
X 2
X 3
− 27.24368
X 1 2
− 29.53629
X 2 2
− 71.96624
X 3 2
Where X1 is the pH, X2 is the incubation time, and X3 is the temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of BRH in PBS buffer solution
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of BRH was carried out in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4) at 25 °C. First, prepare 2 mg mL−1 of CDs-ABT stock solution with water as the solvent, and then add different concentrations of BRH to 200 μL of CDs-ABT solution and dilute to 10 mL with PBS buffer. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were measured at 330 nm excitation. Under the same conditions as above, by adding common metal ions (K+, Na+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+) and possible coexistences in pharmaceutical preparations (GSH, Cys, HCy, Gly, Tyr, Glu, Lac, Mal, Urea), etc. to perform selective experiments, all measurements are repeated three times. The slits of excitation and emission are set to 5 nm.

TEM images (Fig. 1 A and B) reveal that CDs and CDs-ABT are well dispersed and the morphology is approximately spherical. The HRTEM images (insets in Fig. 1A and B) shows that both have a good crystal structure, with a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm. The particle size distribution of CDs and CDs-ABT are displayed in Fig. 1C and D. It can be seen that the average particle size of CDs-ABT is about 9.2 nm, which is slightly larger than that of CDs (about 8.3 nm). It shows that ABT binds well to the surface of CDs.

To further confirm the well coupling of CDs and ABT, characterization of FTIR, XPS and Zeta potential was carried out next. The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 A) were used for identification of functional groups in CDs, ABT and CDs-ABT. In the FT-IR spectrum of ABT, there are characteristic peaks of –NH2 and CN at 3413 cm−1 and 1634 cm−1. The peaks at 3440 cm−1 and 1721 cm−1 in the CDs spectrum are caused by the stretching vibrations of O–H and CO [36]. After modification with ABT, the absorption band of CDs-ABT at 3420 cm−1 is attributable to the stretching vibrations of –NH2 and –OH, and the C–O stretching vibration peak at 1140 cm−1. The absorption peaks of CO and C–N in the amide bond appeared at 1704 cm−1 and 1395 cm−1 [37]. These results indicate that an amidation reaction occurred between CDs and ABT. The chemical compositions of CDs and CDs-ABT were investigated by XPS analysis (Fig. 2B). There are four absorption peaks at 162 eV (S2p), 285 eV (C1s), 402 eV (N1s) and 532 eV (O1s), suggesting that CDs and CDs-ABT are consisted of S, C, N and O elements. The element contents of CDs and CDs-ABT are shown in the inset in Fig. 2B. The S, C, N and O contents of CDs are 3.44%, 62.81%, 3.56% and 30.19%, respectively. After covalently coupling with ABT, the corresponding element contents of CDs-ABT were 4.62%, 63.91%, 5.15% and 26.32%, respectively. The above results indicate that the N and S content of CDs-ABT is higher than that of CDs. Fig. S3 (A-D) is the high-resolution XPS spectra of CDs-ABT C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p. The C1s spectrum (Fig. S3A) can be fitted to the three peaks of CC/C–C at 284.82 eV, C–O/C–N at 285.82 eV, and CO at 288.171 eV [38]. As shown in Fig. S3B, the curve of N1s is fitted to two peaks, including pyridine N (399.89 eV) and pyrrole N (401.53 eV). The O1s spectrum (Fig. S3C) consists of two peaks of CO at 531.71 eV and C–O at 533.10 eV. As shown in Fig. S3D, the curve of S2p is fitted into two peaks, including S2p1/2 (162.82 eV) and S2p3/2 (163.99 eV) [39]. The above results can further indicate the successful modification of ABT on the surface of CDs. In addition, Fig. 2C shows the Zeta potentials of CDs and CDs-ABT in aqueous solutions, which are −15.21 mV and −7.82 mV, respectively. After the surface of CDs is modified with ABT, the zeta potential of CDs-ABT increases due to the decrease of carboxyl groups and the formation of amide bonds [40].

As shown in Fig. 3 A, an obvious absorption peak of CDs-ABT at 333 nm originated from the n-π* transition of surface state. This peak is related to the red shift of the absorption peak of CDs at 320 nm and the blue shift of the absorption peak of ABT at 342 nm, suggesting that the modification of CDs with ABT was successful. The fluorescence spectra of CDs and CDs-ABT aqueous solutions are displayed in Fig. 3B and C. CDs and CDs-ABT both exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence behaviors under excitation at 300–380 nm. This property can be attributed to uneven particle size or different surface defects. CDs show the maximum excitation/emission wavelength at 330/399 nm, and the quantum yield is 25%. CDs-ABT shows the maximum excitation/emission wavelength at 330/425 nm, and the quantum yield is 31%. The measurement of quantum yield is carried out in FLS1000 steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer equipped with a calibration integrating sphere. Among them, solvent water is used as a blank sample for reference measurement. The insets in Fig. 3B and C are fluorescent images of CDs and CDs-ABT under UV light. Compared with CDs, CDs-ABT solution emits more bright blue fluorescence under a UV lamp. Fig. 3D shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum, optimal excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of CDs-ABT. Its maximum excitation peak and maximum emission peak are mirror-symmetrical. As shown in Figs. S4A and S4B, the fluorescence intensity of CDs and CDs-ABT was only slightly reduced after 10 h of continuous irradiation under 365 nm ultraviolet light. The above results indicate that both have good light stability.

A CCD was used to simultaneously check the optimization conditions, main parameters and their interaction. The optimization conditions, main parameters, and their interaction were simultaneously examined using CCD. Table S2 is obtained from the analysis of variance, and further evaluates the impact of important factors and the interaction between the factors. Based on the analysis of variance data, the F value is greater than 0.05 and P value less than 0.05, indicating that the defined model and model terms are meaningful. Furthermore, the consistency of the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9755 and the adjusted R2 = 0.9676 indicating that the experimental data is very consistent with the empirical model. The above results also suggest that the model can well predict the relationship between the response to fluorescence when various conditions interact.

The relationship of the model prediction response and the actual data is shown in Fig. 4 A. It can be seen that the actual data is distributed near the straight line, which indicates that the model established in the experiment has sufficient consistency with the actual data. Fig. 4B is the normal probability plot of the residuals. The points that almost falls on a straight line imply that the error is normally distributed. The relationship between the irregular residual and the number of runs shown in Fig. 4C shows that the experimental model can properly present the actual data. The above results show that the model can be used for the design and optimization of testing conditions.

The three-dimensional response surface graph of Fig. 5 can more intuitively see the effect of any two variables on the fluorescence response (F0–F), and can intuitively find the optimal range. On the response surface affected by the incubation time (Fig. 5A and B), as the temperature increased, the quenching degree of CDs-ABT first increased and then decreased. It can be seen that the quenching degree of CDs-ABT by BRH after 10 min maximum, so the best incubation time is 10 min. Fig. 5B and C reveal the impact of temperature on the fluorescence response (F0–F) in the range of 15–35 °C, and it can be analyzed from the figure that the best detection temperature is about 25 °C. The response surface affected by pH is displayed in Fig. 5A and C. As expected, 7 is the best pH value. In short, the optimal detection time, temperature, and pH value were found to be 10 min, 25 °C and 7, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of CDs-ABT to BRH
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is an important parameter for studying the performance of fluorescence sensing platforms. The fluorescence response of common metal ions (K+, Na+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+) and possible coexistences in pharmaceutical preparations (GSH, Cys, HCy, Gly, Tyr, Glu, Lac, Mal, urea) on CDs-ABT were investigated to evaluate the selectivity of CDs-ABT to BRH. As shown in Fig. 6C, in the absence of BRH, after adding 130 μM common metal ions and coexistents, the relative fluorescence intensity of CDs-ABT is almost unchanged. After adding the same concentration of BRH, the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly, indicating that the high selectivity of CDs-ABT to BRH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of CDs-ABT to BRH
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we studied the sensing mechanism of BRH to CDs-ABT. Compared with the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs-ABT and BRH alone, there is no new UV absorption peak in CDs-ABT + BRH (Fig. 7 A). The above result illustrated that there is no non-luminescent ground state complex formed between CDs-ABT and BRH, so the possibility of static quenching is excluded. Fig. 7B shows that the optimal excitation and emission spectra of CDs-ABT and the absorption spectra of BRH overlap greatly. It is speculated that the fluorescence quenching may be caused by FRET or IFE between the two. As shown in Fig. 7C, the experiment measured that the fluorescence lifetime of CDs-ABT was basically unchanged before and after the addition of 100 μM BRH (the lifetime of CDs was 1.83 ns, and the lifetime after adding BRH was 2.05 ns). The above results indicate that BRH has almost no effect on the fluorescence lifetime of CDs-ABT. Therefore, the FRET mechanism is excluded, and the results indicate that IFE is the sensing mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of BRH in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the feasibility and accuracy of BRH detection by CDs-ABT in practical applications, we carried out a drug sample (Fu fang huang lian su tablets) and a spike recovery experiment of BRH in urine. Take 1 Fu fang huang lian su tablet, carefully remove the sugar coating, and grind its contents. Weigh 10 mg of fine powder into a beaker, add 15 mL of boiling water to dissolve it, cool and filter, transfer the filtrate to a 25 mL volumetric flask and dilute the volume to obtain a stock solution of the drug sample. BRH of different concentrations (20, 40, and 60 μM) were added into the 100 μL drug sample stock solution dissolved in PBS buffer. Detect the concentration of BRH in the drug sample after adding the standard, and measure each concentration three times. Take the urine of healthy adults and filter it through a 0.45 μm disposable pinhole filter to obtain a urine sample backup solution. Then the standard addition and recovery experiment of BRH in the urine sample was carried out, and the specific experimental process was the same as above. Table 2 and Table 3 shows the determination results. The recovery rates of the three groups of drug samples were between 97.6 and 105.0%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was between 1.3 and 2.1%. The spiked recovery rates of the three groups of urine samples ranged from 97.3 to 108.5%, and the RSD was 1.2–4.1%. The above results show that this method is reliable and practical, and can be used for rapid semi-quantitative or qualitative detection of BRH.

The fluorescent nanoprobe CDs-ABT were synthesized by covalently linking CDs and benzothiazole derivatives (ABT) for the selective and quantitative detection of BRH. The prepared CDs-ABT has excellent stability, high water solubility and high quantum yield, and can be used as a nanoprobe to detect BRH with high sensitivity and selectivity. RSM was used to optimize the detection conditions of CDs-ABT on BRH, including the influence of individual factors and their interactions. The linear range for BRH detection was 10–130 μM, and the limit of detection is 3.5 nM. Studies have found that the interaction mechanism between CDs-ABT and BRH is IFE. The reliability of this proposed method has been validated in the BRH test of drug samples and urine. The actual sample analysis results confirmed the feasibility of the method in practical applications.

Fanyong Yan: Formal analysis, conceived and designed the study. analyzed the data. made suggestions for the improvement of the article. All authors approved the manuscript. Zhonghui Sun: Writing – original draft, conceived and designed the study. performed or assisted with experiments. wrote the manuscript. All authors approved the manuscript. Shiying Luo: Formal analysis, performed or assisted with experiments. analyzed the data. made suggestions for the improvement of the article. All authors approved the manuscript. Hao Zhang: Formal analysis, analyzed the data. made suggestions for the improvement of the article. All authors approved the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51678409, 51638011 and 51578375), Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology (19JCYBJC19800, 18JCYBJC87500, 15ZCZDSF00880), State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes (Z1-201507), and the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (TD13-5042).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2021.109446.
